. . . . .

City of National City
Office of the City Attorney
1243 National City Boulevard, National City, CA 92050-4397
George H. Eiser, III—City Attorney                    (619) 336-4220

February 8, 1991


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California  95804

Re:  Request for Reconsideration;
     Your File No.: I-90-559

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     By letter of July 13, 1990, I requested a Commission Opinion
concerning a proposed amendment to the Redevelopment Plan for the
National City Downtown Redevelopment Project.  I was subsequently
advised that my request for an Opinion was rejected, but that my
request would be treated as a request for formal written advice.
On or about October 5, 1990, I received a written response to my
request, prepared by Legal Division counsel Blanca M. Breeze, (your
File No. I-90-559).

     Subsequent to receiving your response, I have discussed with Ms.
Breeze certain issues involved in the aforementioned correspon-
dence.   Our discussions, as well as discussions with the City
Council of National City, have prompted me to prepare this request
for reconsideration.  In pursuing this request, we are concerned by
the far-reaching effects of the earlier written advice on the
participation of National City officials on the process of amending
the redevelopment plan for the National City Downtown Redevelopment
Project.  We believe that based on additional information which is
submitted herein, and with consideration of alternative methods of
dealing with the redevelopment plan amendment, the provisions of
the Political Reform Act can be accommodated in a manner less
impactive upon the participation of National City officials.

     In making this request, we ask that particular attention be focused
upon the issues of "bifurcation" and the "public generally" exception,
discussed in more detail below.

I.   FORESEEABILITY; BIFURCATION

     At pages 7-8 of the advice letter, Ms. Breeze states that "it
is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the acquisition of additional land for the redevelopment area will

February 8, 1991
Page 2

have a financial effect upon the value of the numerous
interests in real property and businesses owned by Mayor
Waters, Vice-Mayor Van Deventer, and Councilmembers Dalla and
Pruitt..." (emphasis added).

In reading this conclusion, it appears significant that the
proposed amendment to the redevelopment plan included an
element pertaining to adding additional land to the project
area. Since the proposed plan amendment includes other actions which do not involve adding land to the project area (e.g. designating "acquisition areas" within existing project area boundaries), the question arises as to
whether these other actions could be considered separately
(i.e. "bifurcated") from the question of adding land to the
project area, so that the question as to whether an official
could participate in a decision would be dependent on the
particular action to be considered. It would appear appropriate to consider the elements of the redevelopment plan on a
situation-by-situation basis, with the official's decision to
participate in a particular element depending on the potential
effect that element only on the official's financial
interests.

II. PUBLIC GENERALLY EXCEPTION

At pages 12-13 of the advice letter, there is a discussion of
the "public generally" exception. It is concluded that this
exception applies to Councilmember Dalla who owns a personal
residence within the redevelopment area. It is further
concluded that the exception does not apply to Mayor Waters
and Councilmembers Van Deventer and Pruitt because the facts
presented "do not indicate that a sufficient number of the
residents of the city own commercial real estate and business
interests in the redevelopment area."

Since receiving the advice letter, I have requested information from the city Planning and Finance Departments as to the
number of owners of commercial real estate and business
interests, as well as residential properties, within the
redevelopment project area. The findings of these departments
are set forth hereinbelow:

A. Number of Commercial and Industrial Buildings in the
   Redevelopment Project Area

   Based upon a survey completed at my request by the
   National City Planning Department, it was determined that
   712 commercial and industrial buildings are located in

February 8, 1991
Page 3


                    the redevelopment project area. A copy of the memorandum
                    from the Planning Department containing this determi-
                    nation is attached hereto as Exhibit "A."

    B.  Number of Residential Buildings within the Redevelopment
        Project Area

        A Planning Department survey of residential structures
        indicates that there are 6,776 single family residences,
        and 8,280 multiple family residences within the City of
        National City. Of these, it is estimated that approxi-
        mately 4,525 single family residences and 5,550 multi-
        family units are within the redevelopment project area.
        A copy of the memorandum from the Planning Department
        containing this determination is attached hereto as
        Exhibit "B."

    C.  Number of Businesses with Sales Tax Permits in the
        Redevelopment Project Area

        In order to further substantiate the number of business
        interests within the redevelopment project area, I
        requested and received a study from the National City
        Finance Department on the number of businesses with sales
        tax permits within the redevelopment project area. The
        Finance Department has determined that there are 1,782
        businesses with sales tax permits located in the project
        area. This number represents 78% of all businesses with
        sales tax permits in the City. A copy of the memorandum
        from the Finance Department setting forth the information
        with regard to sales tax permits is attached hereto as
        Exhibit "C."

III. REAL PROPERTY INTERESTS OF COUNCILWOMAN ROSALIE G. ZARATE

    Since my original request for advice of July 13, 1990, a
    general municipal election was held in National City. As a
    result of that election Councilman Fred Pruitt left office,
    and new Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate assumed office.

    Councilwoman Zarate owns the following interests in real
    property within the City of National City, and within the
    boundaries of the Project Area of the National City Downtown
    Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan: (1) a fee interest
    in real property located at 1735 Plaza Boulevard, acquired in
    1978. The fair market value of the property is greater than
    $100,000. The property is improved with an apartment build-
    ing, which is rented to individual tenants, producing an

February 8, 1991
Page 4


     annual income of over $10,000; (2) a personal residence
located at 1730 East 12th Street, acquired in 1964.  Neither
of Councilwoman Zarate's properties is located within 2,500
feet of any acquisition areas designated in Amendment III of
the Redevelopment Plan, as set forth in my letter of July 13,
1990.

IV. ADDITIONAL INFORMATION REGARDING TENANTS OF COMMERCIAL
BUILDINGS OWNED BY MAYOR GEORGE H. WATERS AND COUNCILMAN JESSE
E. VAN DEVENTER

     At page one of the October 5, 1990 advice letter, it is
stated: "We have insufficient information regarding tenants
of the commercial buildings owned by Mayor George H. Waters
and Vice-Mayor Jesse E. Van Deventer.  Therefore, we treat
your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to
the provisions of Regulation 18329(b)(8)(c)...."

     As part of this request for reconsideration, we would also
request that your advice be in the form of a formal written
advice.  Therefore, we provide the following additional
information concerning the financial interests of Mayor George
H. Waters and Councilman Jesse E. Van Deventer.  (Mr. Van
Deventer was formerly the Vice-Mayor.  Since my letter of July
13, another Vice-Mayor has been appointed, with Mr. Van
Deventer retaining the title of "Councilman.")

A.  Mayor George H. Waters

     Mayor George H. Waters owns the following interests
within the City of National City, and within the bound-
aries of the Project Area of the National City Downtown
Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan.  These inter-
ests were acquired approximately 25 years ago:

     •  A fee interest in real property located at 1436
        Highland Avenue.  The fair market value of the
        property is greater than $100,000.  The property is
        improved with a commercial building, which is
        rented to Dr. Rudolfo Picazo, a dentist, producing
        an annual income to Mr. Waters of between $1,000
        and $10,000.  The lease to Dr. Picazo ends on
        December 31, 1991, and is renewable for an addi-
        tional one year term upon mutual agreement.

     •  A fee interest in real property located at 1440
        Highland Avenue.  The fair market value of the
        property is greater than $100,000.  The property is

February 8, 1991
Page 5


                    improved with a residence, which is rented, produc-
                    ing annual income of between $1,000 and $10,000.

            •       A fee interest in real property located at 1442
                    Highland Avenue. The fair market value of the
                    property is greater than $100,000. The property is
                    improved with a commercial building, which is
                    rented to Maniflo Money Exchange, a money exchange
                    which buys and sells foreign currency, producing
                    annual income to Mr. Waters of between $1,000 and
                    $10,000. The lease to Maniflo Money Exchange ends
                    on December 31, 1991, and is renewable for an
                    additional one year term upon mutual agreement.

            •       A fee interest in real property located at 1444-
                    1446 Highland Avenue. The fair market value of the
                    property is greater than $100,000. The property is
                    improved with a commercial building, which is used
                    as the place of business for a commercial office
                    machines sales and service business of which Mayor
                    Waters is the proprietor.

     B.    Councilman Jessie E. Van Deventer

            Councilman Jessie E. Van Deventer owns the following
            interests within the City of National City, and within
            the boundaries of the Project Area of the National City
            Downtown Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan. These
            interests were acquired from the early 1960's through
            1987.

            •       A fee interest in real property at 110 East 16th
                    Street. The fair market value of the property is
                    greater than $100,000. The property is improved
                    with a commercial building, which is rented to
                    Thompson Lacquer, a retailer of auto finish prod-
                    ucts, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deven-
                    ter in excess of $10,000. The term of the lease is
                    from February 15, 1989 to February 14, 1995.

            •       A fee interest in real property at 34 East 17th
                    Street. The fair market value of the property is
                    greater than $100,000. The property is improved
                    with a commercial building. Suite A is rented to
                    Manuel Acuna, an import auto parts store, producing
                    an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of
                    $10,000. The term of the lease is from November 1,
                    1990 to October 31, 2000. Suites B and C are

February 8, 1991
Page 6


rented to Alba Rodriguez, who operates a party
rental business and an auto body and repair busi-
ness, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deven-
ter in excesses of $10,000. The term of the lease is
from October 1, 1990 to September 30, 1992.

•	A fee interest in real property at 106 East 17th
Street. The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000. The property is improved
with a commercial building, which is rented to
Dobson Welding, a welding shop, producing an annual
income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000.
The term of the lease is from October 1, 1990 to
September 30, 2000.

•	A fee interest in real property at 25 East 17th
Street. The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000. The property is improved
with a commercial building, which is rented to Bush
Power Brake Service, a truck and automotive brake
repair business, producing an annual income to Mr.
an Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term of the
lease is from February 15, 1985 to February 14,
1995.

•	A fee interest in real property at 115 East 17th
Street. The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000. The property is improved
with a commercial building. Suite A is rented to
Brake World, a truck and automotive brake repair
business, producing an annual income to Mr. Van
Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term of the
lease is from April 15, 1990 to April 14, 1993.
Suite B is rented to Safelite Glass, an auto glass
service and repair business, producing an annual
income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000.
The term of the lease is from September 1, 1988 to
August 31, 1993. Suite C is rented to Trini's Auto
Electric, an auto electric repair business, produc-
ing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer of approx-
imately $10,000. The term of the lease is from
September 15, 1988 to September 14, 1993. Suite D
is rented to Ted's Upholstery, an upholstery shop,
producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer of
approximately $10,000. The term of the lease is
from February 1, 1990 to January 31, 1993.

February 8, 1991
Page 7


•	A fee interest in real property at 125 East 17th Street, improved with a commercial structure and having a fair market value of over $100,000. This property is used as the place of business for Vancon Corporation, a closely held corporation of which Councilman Van Deventer is the majority stockholder. The property is leased to Vancon Corporation, generating income to Mr. Van Deventer of approximately $5,000 annually. The term of the lease is from July 1, 1989 to June 30, 1992. A portion of this property is also leased on a month to month basis to Marin Income Tax Service, an income tax service, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer of approximately $5,000.

•	A fee interest in real property at 41 East 18th Street. The fair market value of the property is greater than $100,000. The property is improved with a commercial building, which is rented to J.D. Brothers Auto Repair, an auto repair business, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term of the lease is from February 1, 1988 to December 15, 1991.

•	A fee interest in real property at 115 East 18th Street. The fair market value of the property is greater than $100,000. The property is improved with a commercial building, which is rented to Prochem Specialty Products, a wholesaler of cleaning solutions, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term of the lease is from November 1, 1990 to October 31, 1993.

In addition to the above real property interests, Councilman Van Deventer has the following financial interests:

•	During 1989 Wirtz Tile Company, Inc. was granted authority to draw upon a line of credit held in the name of Mr. Van Deventer and his wife at San Diego National Bank. The draw against said line of credit was approximately $75,000.00 in total during 1989. Wirtz Tile makes all payments due to San Diego National Bank stemming from said draw. Mr. Van Deventer's interest in the Wirtz business is solely beneficial and as security for said draw. The total consideration paid to Mr. Van Deventer from Wirtz Tile Company, Inc. was $2,000. Wirtz

February 8, 1991
Page 8


Tile Company, Inc. is located at 1533 Wilson Ave-
nue, National City, within the redevelopment pro-
ject area.

•	Mr. Van Deventer is the beneficiary of a note with
a current balance of approximately $5,000, secured
by a second deed of trust on a residence located at
2965 Ridgeway Drive, National City, which is out-
side the boundaries of the redevelopment project
and beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed new police
facility site. The trustor under the deed of trust
and obligor on the note is Juan Lopez, who owns an
automotive machine shop at 1526 East 18th Street,
National City, located within the redevelopment
project area. Mr. Van Deventer receives a monthly
payment of $108 under the note.

•	Mr. Van Deventer is the obligee under a promissory
note in the amount of $52,178.56, executed by Nancy
Thompson, owner of three child care facilities in
National City. The child care facilities are
located at 2207 Highland Avenue, 2200 "E" Avenue,
and 1540 Harrison Avenue, National City. The
Highland Avenue and "E" Avenue locations are within
the boundaries of the redevelopment project area.
The Harrison Avenue location is outside the rede-
velopment project area. Mr. Van Deventer received
approximately $3,500 in payment of this note in
1990.

•	Mr. Van Deventer is the obligee under a promissory
note in the amount of $11,889.55, executed by
Eugenio Trinidad, also a lessee of Mr. Van
Deventer's and the operator of an auto electric
repair business at 115 East 17th Street, Suite C,
National City. This location is within the rede-
velopment project area. This note is secured by
the inventory of Mr. Trinidad's business. Mr. Van
Deventer received $753.61 in income under this note
in 1990.

V. ISSUES PRESENTED

Are Mayor Waters, Councilman Van Deventer, or Councilwoman
Zarate precluded from participating in action(s) to amend the
National City Downtown Redevelopment Project Redevelopment
Plan, by virtue of the financial interests discussed above,
and in view of the provisions of Section 18702(c) (the "Public

February 8, 1991
Page 9


Generally Exception"), of the Regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission?

More specifically, are the above officials subject to a
"blanket disqualification" due to their financial interests in
the Redevelopment Project Area, or may their participation be
based on the specific amendment to the Redevelopment Plan
being considered, i.e., taking the concept of "bifurcation"
into account?

Very truly yours,

[signature]

George H. Eiser, III
City Attorney

GHE/sbc

Acting General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 "J" Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, California 95804 Re: Request for Reconsideration; Your File No.: I-90-559 Dear Mr. Hallabrin: By letter of July 13, 1990, I requested a Commission Opinion concerning a proposed amendment to the Redevelopment Plan for the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project. I was subsequently advised that my request for an Opinion was rejected, but that my request would be treated as a request for formal written advice. On or about October 5, 1990, I received a written response to my request, prepared by Legal Division counsel Blanca M. Breeze, (your File No. I-90-559). Subsequent to receiving your response, I have discussed with Ms. Breeze certain issues involved in the aforementioned correspon- dence. Our discussions, as well as discussions with the City Council of National City, have prompted me to prepare this request for reconsideration. In pursuing this request, we are concerned by the far-reaching effects of the earlier written advice on the participation of National City officials on the process of amending the redevelopment plan for the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project. We believe that based on additional information which is submitted herein, and with consideration of alternative methods of dealing with the redevelopment plan amendment, the provisions of the Political Reform Act can be accommodated in a manner less impactive upon the participation of National City officials. In making this request, we ask that particular attention be focused upon the issues of "bifurcation" and the "public generally" exception, discussed in more detail below. I. FORESEEABILITY; BIFURCATION At pages 7-8 of the advice letter, Ms. Breeze states that "it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the acquisition of additional land for the redevelopment area will February 8, 1991 Page 2 have a financial effect upon the value of the numerous interests in real property and businesses owned by Mayor Waters, Vice-Mayor Van Deventer, and Councilmembers Dalla and Pruitt..." (emphasis added). In reading this conclusion, it appears significant that the proposed amendment to the redevelopment plan included an element pertaining to adding additional land to the project area. Since the proposed plan amendment includes other actions which do not involve adding land to the project area (e.g. designating "acquisition areas" within existing project area boundaries), the question arises as to whether these other actions could be considered separately (i.e. "bifurcated") from the question of adding land to the project area, so that the question as to whether an official could participate in a decision would be dependent on the particular action to be considered. It would appear appropriate to consider the elements of the redevelopment plan on a situation-by-situation basis, with the official's decision to participate in a particular element depending on the potential effect that element only on the official's financial interests. II. PUBLIC
